dn33:1.1.2: that on one occasion the Blessed One was wandering among the Mallans with a large Saṅgha of monks—approximately 500 monks in all—and arrived at Pāvā, a city of the Mallans.
dn33:1.1.3: There he stayed near Pāvā in Cunda the silversmith’s mango grove.
dn33:1.2.1: Now, at that time a new reception hall named Ubbhataka had just been built by the Pāvā Mallans, and it had not yet been dwelled in by any contemplative, brahman, or anyone at all in human form.
dn33:1.2.2: The Pāvā Mallans heard, “The Blessed One, they say, wandering among the Mallans with a large Saṅgha of monks—approximately 500 monks in all—has arrived at Pāvā and is staying near Pāvā in Cunda the silversmith’s mango grove.”
dn33:1.2.3: So the Pāvā Mallans went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As they were sitting there they said to him,
dn33:1.2.4: “Lord, a new reception hall named Ubbhataka has just been built by the Pāvā Mallans, and it has not yet been dwelled in by any contemplative, brahman, or anyone at all in human form.
dn33:1.2.5: May the Blessed One be the first to use it. When the Blessed One has used it first, the Pāvā Mallans will use it afterwards.
dn33:1.2.6: That will be for their long-term welfare & happiness.”
dn33:1.2.7: The Blessed One acquiesced with silence.
dn33:1.3.1: Sensing his acquiescence, the Pāvā Mallans got up from their seats, bowed down to him, circumambulated him, and then went to the new reception hall. On arrival, they spread it all over with felt rugs, arranged seats, set out a water vessel, and raised an oil lamp.
dn33:1.3.2: Then they went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, stood to one side.
dn33:1.3.3: As they were standing there they said to him,
dn33:1.3.4: “Lord, the reception hall has been covered all over with felt rugs, seats have been arranged, a water vessel has been set out, and an oil lamp raised.
dn33:1.3.5: It is now time for the Blessed One to do as he sees fit.”
dn33:1.4.1: So the Blessed One—after adjusting his under robe and carrying his bowl & outer robe—went together with the Saṅgha of monks to the reception hall. On arrival he washed his feet, entered the hall, and sat with his back to the central post, facing east.
dn33:1.4.2: The Saṅgha of monks washed their feet, entered the hall, and sat with their backs to the western wall, facing east, ranged around the Blessed One.
dn33:1.4.3: The Pāvā Mallans washed their feet, entered the hall, and sat with their backs to the eastern wall, facing west, ranged around the Blessed One.
dn33:1.4.4: Then the Blessed One—having spent most of the night instructing, urging, rousing, & encouraging the Pāvā Mallans with a Dhamma talk—dismissed them, saying,
dn33:1.4.6: Do what you think it is now time to do.”
dn33:1.4.7: Responding, “As you say, lord,” to the Blessed One, the Pāvā Mallans got up from their seats and, bowing down to him & circumambulating him, left.
dn33:1.5.1: Then, not long after the Pāvā Mallans had left, the Blessed One—surveying the silent Saṅgha of monks—addressed Ven. Sāriputta,
dn33:1.5.2: “Sāriputta, the Saṅgha of monks is free of drowsiness.
dn33:1.5.3: Let yourself be inspired by a Dhamma talk for them.
dn33:1.5.6: “As you say, lord,” Ven. Sāriputta responded to the Blessed One.
dn33:1.5.7: Then the Blessed One, having had his outer robe arranged folded in four, lay down on his right side in the lion’s sleeping posture, with one foot on top of the other, mindful & alert, having made a mental note to get up.
dn33:1.6.1: Now, on that occasion Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta had recently died in Pāvā. On his death, the Nigaṇṭhas had split into two factions.
dn33:1.6.2: They kept arguing, quarreling, & disputing, stabbing one another with weapons of the mouth:
dn33:1.6.3: “You don’t understand this Dhamma-Vinaya. I understand this Dhamma-Vinaya. How could you understand this Dhamma-Vinaya? You practice wrongly. I practice rightly. I’m consistent. You’re inconsistent. What should be said first, you said last. What should be said last, you said first. What you cogitated so long has been turned inside out. Your position has been overthrown. You’re trapped, so go try to release your position or disentangle yourself if you can!”
dn33:1.6.4: Nothing but slaughter, as it were, was being committed among the Nigaṇṭha Nāṭa-sons.
dn33:1.6.5: Even the lay disciples of Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta, dressed in white, seemed disenchanted, disgusted, & repelled by the Nigaṇṭha Nāṭa-sons, and at how that poorly proclaimed Dhamma-Vinaya was poorly expounded, not leading out, not conducive to calming, expounded by one who was not rightly self-awakened, its foundation [now] broken, without an arbiter.
dn33:1.7.1: So Ven. Sāriputta addressed the monks,
dn33:1.7.2: “Friends, Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta has recently died in Pāvā.
dn33:1.7.3: On his death, the Nigaṇṭhas have split into two factions.… Even the lay disciples of Nigaṇṭha Nāṭaputta, dressed in white, seem disenchanted, disgusted, & repelled by the Nigaṇṭha Nāṭa-sons, and at how that poorly proclaimed Dhamma-Vinaya was poorly expounded, not leading out, not conducive to calming, expounded by one who was not rightly self-awakened, its foundation [now] broken, without an arbiter.
dn33:1.7.4: “That’s the way it is, friends, with a poorly proclaimed Dhamma-Vinaya, poorly expounded, not leading out, not conducive to calming, expounded by one who is not rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.7.5: But our Dhamma of the Blessed One is well expounded, leading out, conducive to calming, expounded by one who is rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.7.6: So it should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time. That will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:1.7.7: “And which is our Dhamma of the Blessed One that is well expounded, leading out, conducive to calming, expounded by one who is rightly self-awakened,
dn33:1.7.8: that should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings?
dn33:1.7.10: “There is, friends, one dhamma that is rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.7.11: That should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:1.8.4: “This, friends, is one dhamma that is rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.8.5: That should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:1.9.1: “There are, friends, two dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.9.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:1.9.6: “The view of becoming & the view of non-becoming.
dn33:1.9.11: “Skill in (knowing) offenses & skill in exiting from offenses.
dn33:1.9.12: “Skill in (concentration) attainments & skill in exiting from attainments.
dn33:1.9.14: “Skill in (knowing) sense media & skill in (knowing) dependent co-arising.
dn33:1.9.15: “Skill in (knowing) what is possible & skill in (knowing) what is impossible.
dn33:1.9.22: “Unguarded sense doors & not knowing moderation in eating.
dn33:1.9.23: “Guarded sense doors & knowing moderation in eating.
dn33:1.9.27: “The theme of tranquility & the theme of exertion.
dn33:1.9.32: “Purity of view & the exertion of one with such a view.
dn33:1.9.33: “Terror with regard to the causes for terror & appropriate exertion in one who feels terror.
dn33:1.9.34: “Discontent with regard to skillful qualities & unrelenting exertion.3
dn33:1.9.36: “Knowledge with regard to ending (of effluents) & knowledge of (their) non-recurrence.
dn33:1.9.37: “These, friends, are the two dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.9.38: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:1.10.1: “There are, friends, three dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.10.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:1.10.5: greed as a root of what is unskillful, aversion as a root of what is unskillful, delusion as a root of what is unskillful.
dn33:1.10.7: lack of greed as a root of what is skillful, lack of aversion as a root of what is skillful, lack of delusion as a root of what is skillful.
dn33:1.10.9: bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, mental misconduct.
dn33:1.10.11: good bodily conduct, good verbal conduct, good mental conduct.
dn33:1.10.13: a thought of sensuality, a thought of ill will, a thought of harmfulness.
dn33:1.10.15: a thought of renunciation, a thought of non-ill will, a thought of harmlessness.
dn33:1.10.17: a resolve on sensuality, a resolve on ill will, a resolve on harmfulness.
dn33:1.10.19: a resolve on renunciation, a resolve on non-ill will, a resolve on harmlessness.
dn33:1.10.21: a perception of sensuality, a perception of ill will, a perception of harmfulness.
dn33:1.10.23: a perception of renunciation, a perception of non-ill will, a perception of harmlessness.
dn33:1.10.25: the property of sensuality, the property of ill will, the property of harmfulness.
dn33:1.10.27: the property of renunciation, the property of non-ill will, the property of harmlessness.
dn33:1.10.29: the property of sensuality, the property of form, the property of formlessness.
dn33:1.10.31: the property of form, the property of formlessness, the property of cessation.5
dn33:1.10.33: the low property, the middling property, the exquisite property.6
dn33:1.10.35: craving for sensuality, craving for becoming, craving for non-becoming.
dn33:1.10.37: craving for sensuality, craving for form, craving for the formless.
dn33:1.10.39: craving for form, craving for the formless, craving for cessation.7
dn33:1.10.41: self-identity view, uncertainty, grasping at habits & practices.
dn33:1.10.43: the effluent of sensuality, the effluent of becoming, the effluent of ignorance.8
dn33:1.10.47: the search for sensuality, the search for becoming, the search for a holy life.
dn33:1.10.49: the ‘I-am-better’ type, the ‘I-am-the-same’ type, the ‘I-am-lower’ type.
dn33:1.10.51: the extent of the past, the extent of the future, the extent of the present.
dn33:1.10.53: the limit of self-identity, the limit of the origination of self-identity, the limit of the cessation of self-identity.
dn33:1.10.55: a feeling of pleasure, a feeling of pain, a feeling of neither pleasure nor pain.
dn33:1.10.57: the stressfulness of pain, the stressfulness of fabrication, the stressfulness of change.
dn33:1.10.59: the accumulation of wrongness with a certain result, the accumulation of rightness with a certain result, an uncertain result.
dn33:1.10.61: One doubts, is uncertain, is undecided, & is unconfident about the past…
dn33:1.10.63: or about the present right now.
dn33:1.10.64: “Three things the Tathāgata doesn’t have to protect against:
dn33:1.10.65: The Tathāgata, friends, is one of pure bodily conduct.
dn33:1.10.66: He has no bodily misconduct that he would have to protect (with the thought), ‘Don’t let anyone else know this of me.’ The Tathāgata is one of pure verbal conduct.
dn33:1.10.67: He has no verbal misconduct that he would have to protect (with the thought), ‘Don’t let anyone else know this of me.’ The Tathāgata is one of pure mental conduct.
dn33:1.10.68: He has no mental misconduct that he would have to protect (with the thought), ‘Don’t let anyone else know this of me.’
dn33:1.10.70: the impediment of passion, the impediment of aversion, the impediment of delusion.
dn33:1.10.72: the fire of passion, the fire of aversion, the fire of delusion.
dn33:1.10.74: the fire of those to be revered, the fire of the householder, the fire of those worthy of offerings.
dn33:1.10.76: form with a surface & offering resistance, form with no surface & offering resistance, form with no surface & offering no resistance.
dn33:1.10.78: a meritorious fabrication, a demeritorious fabrication, an imperturbable fabrication.
dn33:1.10.80: the person in training [a stream-winner through a non-returner], the person beyond training [an arahant], and the person neither in training nor beyond training.
dn33:1.10.82: the elder through birth, the elder through Dhamma, the elder through convention.
dn33:1.10.83: “Three grounds for the making of merit:
dn33:1.10.84: the ground for the making of merit composed of giving, the ground for the making of merit composed of virtue, the ground for the making of merit composed of development [of immeasurable goodwill].
dn33:1.10.86: through what is seen, through what is heard, through what is suspected.
dn33:1.10.88: There are, friends, beings who desire sensual pleasures as they present themselves. They exercise power with regard to sensual pleasures as they present themselves—such as human beings, some devas, & some beings in lower destinations.
dn33:1.10.89: This is the first sensual-pleasure rebirth.
dn33:1.10.90: There are beings, friends, who desire what they have created. They exercise power with regard to sensual pleasures, having created them again & again—such as the Devas Who Delight in Creation.
dn33:1.10.91: This is the second sensual-pleasure rebirth.
dn33:1.10.92: There are beings, friends, who desire what is created by others. They exercise power with regard to sensual pleasures created by others—such as the Devas Who Wield Power over the Creations of Others.
dn33:1.10.93: This is the third sensual-pleasure rebirth.
dn33:1.10.95: There are, friends, beings who, having given rise to bliss again & again, dwell in bliss—such as the devas of Brahmā’s retinue.
dn33:1.10.96: This is the first blissful rebirth.
dn33:1.10.97: There are, friends, beings who are permeated & pervaded, suffused & filled with bliss,
dn33:1.10.98: so that every now & then they exclaim,
dn33:1.10.99: ‘O, bliss! O, bliss!’—such as the Radiant Devas.
dn33:1.10.100: This is the second blissful rebirth.
dn33:1.10.101: There are, friends, beings who are permeated & pervaded, suffused & filled with bliss.
dn33:1.10.102: They, peacefully, are content, sensitive to mental bliss—such as the Beautiful Black devas.
dn33:1.10.103: This is the third blissful rebirth.
dn33:1.10.105: the discernment of a person in training, the discernment of a person beyond training, and the discernment of a person neither in training nor beyond training.
dn33:1.10.107: discernment acquired through learning, discernment acquired through contemplating, discernment acquired through (mental) development.
dn33:1.10.109: the weapon of learning, the weapon of seclusion, the weapon of discernment.
dn33:1.10.111: the ‘I-will-come-to-know-the-unknown’ faculty, the knowledge faculty, the having-knowledge faculty.
dn33:1.10.113: the eye of the flesh, the divine eye, the eye of discernment.
dn33:1.10.115: the training in heightened virtue, the training in heightened mind, the training in heightened discernment.
dn33:1.10.117: development of body, development of mind, development of discernment.
dn33:1.10.119: unsurpassed vision, unsurpassed practice, unsurpassed release.
dn33:1.10.121: concentration with directed thought & evaluation, concentration without directed thought & with a modicum of evaluation, concentration without directed thought & evaluation.
dn33:1.10.123: emptiness concentration, themeless [or: signless] concentration, undirected concentration.
dn33:1.10.125: bodily purity, verbal purity, mental purity.
dn33:1.10.127: bodily sagacity, verbal sagacity, mental sagacity.
dn33:1.10.129: skillfulness in (knowing about) going forward, skillfulness in (knowing about) decline, skillfulness in tactics.
dn33:1.10.131: intoxication with health, intoxication with youth, intoxication with life.
dn33:1.10.133: the self as a governing principle, the world as a governing principle, the Dhamma as a governing principle.
dn33:1.10.135: One might converse about the past, (saying,)
dn33:1.10.136: ‘That’s how it was in the past.’
dn33:1.10.137: One might converse about the future, (saying,)
dn33:1.10.138: ‘That’s how it will be in the future.’
dn33:1.10.139: One might converse about the present, (saying,)
dn33:1.10.140: ‘That’s how it is in the present.’
dn33:1.10.142: the clear knowing of the knowledge of previous lifetimes, the clear knowing of the knowledge of the passing away & arising of beings, the clear knowing of the knowledge of the ending of the effluents.
dn33:1.10.144: a divine dwelling, a brahmā-dwelling, a noble dwelling.
dn33:1.10.146: the marvel of psychic power, the marvel of telepathy, the marvel of instruction.
dn33:1.10.147: “These, friends, are the three dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.10.148: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:1.11.1: “There are, friends, four dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.11.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:1.11.5: There is the case, friends, where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
dn33:1.11.6: He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
dn33:1.11.7: He remains focused on the mind in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
dn33:1.11.8: He remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
dn33:1.11.10: There is the case, friends, where a monk generates desire, endeavors, activates persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the sake of the non-arising of evil, unskillful qualities that have not yet arisen.
dn33:1.11.11: He generates desire, endeavors, activates persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the sake of the abandoning of evil, unskillful qualities that have arisen.
dn33:1.11.12: He generates desire, endeavors, activates persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the sake of the arising of skillful qualities that have not yet arisen.
dn33:1.11.13: He generates desire, endeavors, activates persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the maintenance, non-confusion, increase, plenitude, development, & culmination of skillful qualities that have arisen.
dn33:1.11.15: There is the case, friends, where a monk develops the base of power endowed with concentration founded on desire & the fabrications of exertion.
dn33:1.11.16: He develops the base of power endowed with concentration founded on persistence & the fabrications of exertion.
dn33:1.11.17: He develops the base of power endowed with concentration founded on intent & the fabrications of exertion.
dn33:1.11.18: He develops the base of power endowed with concentration founded on discrimination & the fabrications of exertion.
dn33:1.11.20: There is the case, friends, where a monk—quite secluded from sensuality, secluded from unskillful qualities—enters & remains in the first jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of seclusion, accompanied by directed thought & evaluation.
dn33:1.11.21: With the stilling of directed thoughts & evaluations, he enters & remains in the second jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of concentration, unification of awareness free from directed thought & evaluation—internal assurance.
dn33:1.11.22: With the fading of rapture, he remains equanimous, mindful, & alert, and senses pleasure with the body. He enters & remains in the third jhāna, of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous & mindful, one has a pleasant abiding.’
dn33:1.11.23: With the abandoning of pleasure & pain—as with the earlier disappearance of elation & distress—he enters & remains in the fourth jhāna: purity of equanimity & mindfulness, neither pleasure nor pain.
dn33:1.11.25: There is, friends, the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to a pleasant abiding in the here & now.
dn33:1.11.26: There is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to the attainment of knowledge & vision.
dn33:1.11.27: There is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to mindfulness & alertness.
dn33:1.11.28: There is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to the ending of the effluents.
dn33:1.11.29: “And which is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to a pleasant abiding in the here & now?
dn33:1.11.30: There is the case where a monk—quite secluded from sensuality, secluded from unskillful qualities—enters & remains in the first jhāna…
dn33:1.11.31: the second jhāna… the third jhāna…
dn33:1.11.32: the fourth jhāna: purity of equanimity & mindfulness, neither pleasure nor pain.
dn33:1.11.33: This is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to a pleasant abiding in the here & now.
dn33:1.11.34: “And which is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to the attainment of knowledge & vision?
dn33:1.11.35: There is the case where a monk attends to the perception of light and is resolved on the perception of daytime (at any hour of the day). Day (for him) is the same as night, night is the same as day.
dn33:1.11.36: By means of an awareness open & unhampered, he develops a brightened mind.
dn33:1.11.37: This is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to the attainment of knowledge & vision.
dn33:1.11.38: “And which is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to mindfulness & alertness?
dn33:1.11.39: There is the case where feelings are known to the monk as they arise, known as they persist, known as they subside.
dn33:1.11.40: Perceptions are known to him as they arise, known as they persist, known as they subside.
dn33:1.11.41: Thoughts are known to him as they arise, known as they persist, known as they subside.
dn33:1.11.42: This is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to mindfulness & alertness.
dn33:1.11.43: “And which is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to the ending of the effluents?
dn33:1.11.44: There is the case where a monk remains focused on arising & falling away with reference to the five clinging-aggregates:
dn33:1.11.45: ‘Such is form, such its origination, such its passing away.
dn33:1.11.46: Such is feeling, such its origination, such its passing away.
dn33:1.11.47: Such is perception, such its origination, such its passing away.
dn33:1.11.48: Such are fabrications, such their origination, such their passing away.
dn33:1.11.49: Such is consciousness, such its origination, such its passing away.’
dn33:1.11.50: This is the development of concentration that, when developed & pursued, leads to the ending of the effluents.
dn33:1.11.52: There is the case, friends, where a monk keeps pervading the first direction [the east] with an awareness imbued with goodwill, likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. Thus above, below, & all around, everywhere, in its entirety, he keeps pervading the all-encompassing cosmos with an awareness imbued with goodwill—abundant, enlarged, immeasurable, without hostility, without ill will.
dn33:1.11.53: He keeps pervading the first direction with an awareness imbued with compassion…
dn33:1.11.55: equanimity, likewise the second, likewise the third, likewise the fourth. Thus above, below, & all around, everywhere, in its entirety, he keeps pervading the all-encompassing cosmos with an awareness imbued with equanimity—abundant, enlarged, immeasurable, without hostility, without ill will.
dn33:1.11.57: There is the case, friends, where a monk—with the complete transcending of perceptions of (physical) form, with the disappearance of perceptions of resistance, and not attending to perceptions of multiplicity, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite space’—enters & remains in the dimension of the infinitude of space.
dn33:1.11.58: With the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of space, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite consciousness,’ he enters & remains in the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness.
dn33:1.11.59: With the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness, (perceiving,) ‘There is nothing,’ he enters & remains in the dimension of nothingness.
dn33:1.11.60: With the complete transcending of the dimension of nothingness, he enters & remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.
dn33:1.11.62: There is the case, friends, where a monk, contemplating one thing, associates with it; contemplating one thing, acquiesces to it; contemplating one thing, avoids it; contemplating one thing, subdues it.
dn33:1.11.64: There is the case, friends, where a monk is content with any old robe cloth at all. He speaks in praise of being content with any old robe cloth at all. He doesn’t, for the sake of robe cloth, do anything unseemly or inappropriate. Not getting cloth, he isn’t agitated. Getting cloth, he uses it unattached to it, uninfatuated, guiltless, seeing the drawbacks (of attachment to it), and discerning the escape from them. He doesn’t, on account of his contentment with any old robe cloth at all, exalt himself or disparage others.
dn33:1.11.65: Any monk who is diligent, deft, alert, & mindful in this is said to be a monk standing firm in the ancient, original traditions of the noble ones.
dn33:1.11.66: “And further, the monk is content with any old almsfood at all. He speaks in praise of being content with any old almsfood at all. He doesn’t, for the sake of almsfood, do anything unseemly or inappropriate. Not getting almsfood, he isn’t agitated. Getting almsfood, he uses it unattached to it, uninfatuated, guiltless, seeing the drawbacks (of attachment to it), and discerning the escape from them. He doesn’t, on account of his contentment with any old almsfood at all, exalt himself or disparage others.
dn33:1.11.67: Any monk who is diligent, deft, alert, & mindful in this is said to be a monk standing firm in the ancient, original traditions of the noble ones.
dn33:1.11.68: “And further, the monk is content with any old lodging at all. He speaks in praise of being content with any old lodging at all. He doesn’t, for the sake of lodging, do anything unseemly or inappropriate. Not getting lodging, he isn’t agitated. Getting lodging, he uses it unattached to it, uninfatuated, guiltless, seeing the drawbacks (of attachment to it), and discerning the escape from them. He doesn’t, on account of his contentment with any old lodging at all, exalt himself or disparage others.
dn33:1.11.69: Any monk who is diligent, deft, alert, & mindful in this is said to be a monk standing firm in the ancient, original traditions of the noble ones.
dn33:1.11.70: “And further, the monk finds pleasure & delight in developing (skillful qualities), finds pleasure & delight in abandoning (unskillful qualities). He doesn’t, on account of his pleasure & delight in developing & abandoning, exalt himself or disparage others.
dn33:1.11.71: Any monk who is diligent, deft, alert, & mindful in this is said to be a monk standing firm in the ancient, original traditions of the noble ones.
dn33:1.11.73: the exertion to guard, the exertion to abandon, the exertion to develop, & the exertion to maintain.
dn33:1.11.74: “And which, friends, is the exertion to guard?
dn33:1.11.75: There is the case where a monk, on seeing a form via the eye, doesn’t grasp at any theme or variations by which—
dn33:1.11.76: if he were to dwell without restraint over the faculty of the eye—evil, unskillful qualities such as greed or distress might assail him. He practices with restraint. He guards the faculty of the eye. He achieves restraint with regard to the faculty of the eye.
dn33:1.11.83: This is called the exertion to guard.
dn33:1.11.84: “And which is the exertion to abandon?
dn33:1.11.85: There is the case where a monk doesn’t acquiesce to a thought of sensuality that has arisen (in him). He abandons it, destroys it, dispels it, wipes it out of existence.
dn33:1.11.86: He doesn’t acquiesce to a thought of ill will…
dn33:1.11.88: any evil, unskillful qualities that have arisen (in him). He abandons them, destroys them, dispels them, wipes them out of existence.
dn33:1.11.89: This is called the exertion to abandon.
dn33:1.11.90: “And which is the exertion to develop?
dn33:1.11.91: There is the case where a monk develops mindfulness as a factor for awakening dependent on seclusion, dependent on dispassion, dependent on cessation, resulting in letting go.
dn33:1.11.97: equanimity as a factor for awakening dependent on seclusion, dependent on dispassion, dependent on cessation, resulting in letting go.
dn33:1.11.98: This is called the exertion to develop.
dn33:1.11.99: “And which is the exertion to maintain?
dn33:1.11.100: There is the case where a monk maintains a favorable theme of concentration—the skeleton perception, the worm-eaten perception, the livid perception, the festering perception, the falling-apart perception, the bloated perception.
dn33:1.11.101: This is called the exertion to maintain.
dn33:1.11.103: knowledge with regard to the Dhamma, knowledge with regard to inference, knowledge of limitations, knowledge of conventions.
dn33:1.11.105: knowledge with regard to stress, knowledge with regard to the origination of stress, knowledge with regard to the cessation of stress, knowledge with regard to the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress.
dn33:1.11.107: association with people of integrity, hearing the True Dhamma, appropriate attention, practice of the Dhamma in accordance with the Dhamma.
dn33:1.11.109: There is the case where a disciple of the noble ones is endowed with verified confidence in the Awakened One:
dn33:1.11.110: ‘Indeed, the Blessed One is worthy and rightly self-awakened, consummate in knowledge & conduct, well-gone, an expert with regard to the world, unexcelled as a trainer for those people fit to be tamed, the Teacher of divine & human beings, awakened, blessed.’
dn33:1.11.111: “He/she is endowed with verified confidence in the Dhamma:
dn33:1.11.112: ‘The Dhamma is well-expounded by the Blessed One, to be seen here & now, timeless, inviting verification, pertinent, to be realized by the wise for themselves.’
dn33:1.11.113: “He/she is endowed with verified confidence in the Saṅgha:
dn33:1.11.114: ‘The Saṅgha of the Blessed One’s disciples who have practiced well… who have practiced straight-forwardly… who have practiced methodically… who have practiced masterfully—in other words, the four types of noble disciples when taken as pairs, the eight when taken as individual types—they are the Saṅgha of the Blessed One’s disciples: worthy of gifts, worthy of hospitality, worthy of offerings, worthy of respect, the incomparable field of merit for the world.’
dn33:1.11.115: “He/she is endowed with virtues that are appealing to the noble ones: untorn, unbroken, unspotted, unsplattered, liberating, praised by the wise, untarnished, leading to concentration.
dn33:1.11.117: the fruit of stream-entry, the fruit of once-returning, the fruit of non-returning, the fruit of arahantship.
dn33:1.11.119: the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, the wind property.
dn33:1.11.121: physical food, gross or refined; contact as the second; intellectual intention the third; and consciousness the fourth.
dn33:1.11.123: Consciousness, friends, when standing, stands attached to form, supported by form (as its object), landing on form, watered with delight, and exhibits growth, increase, & proliferation.
dn33:1.11.124: When standing, it stands attached to feeling…
dn33:1.11.126: to fabrications, supported by fabrications (as its object), landing on fabrications, watered with delight, and exhibits growth, increase, & proliferation.34
dn33:1.11.128: One goes off course through desire. One goes off course through aversion. One goes off course through delusion. One goes off course through fear.
dn33:1.11.130: Either with robes as a cause, craving—when arising in a monk— arises,
dn33:1.11.131: or with almsfood as a cause…
dn33:1.11.132: or with lodgings as a cause…
dn33:1.11.133: or with becoming & not-becoming as a cause, craving—when arising in a monk—arises.
dn33:1.11.135: painful practice with slow intuition, painful practice with quick intuition, pleasant practice with slow intuition, pleasant practice with quick intuition.35
dn33:1.11.137: intolerant practice, tolerant practice, self-controlled practice, even practice.36
dn33:1.11.139: the non-greed Dhamma pathway, the non-ill will Dhamma pathway, the right mindfulness Dhamma pathway, the right concentration Dhamma pathway.
dn33:1.11.141: There is, friends, the taking on of a practice that is pleasant in the present but results in pain in the future.
dn33:1.11.142: There is the taking on of a practice that is painful in the present and results in pain in the future.
dn33:1.11.143: There is the taking on of a practice that is painful in the present but results in pleasure in the future.
dn33:1.11.144: There is the taking on of a practice that is pleasant in the present and results in pleasure in the future.
dn33:1.11.146: the virtue aggregate, the concentration aggregate, the discernment aggregate, the release aggregate.
dn33:1.11.148: the strength of persistence, the strength of mindfulness, the strength of concentration, the strength of discernment.
dn33:1.11.150: determination on discernment, determination on truth, determination on relinquishment, determination on stilling.
dn33:1.11.152: the question to be answered categorically, the question to be answered with cross-questioning, the question to be answered analytically, the question to be put aside.
dn33:1.11.154: Friends, there is kamma that is dark with dark result.
dn33:1.11.155: There is kamma that is bright with bright result.
dn33:1.11.156: There is kamma that is dark & bright with dark & bright result.
dn33:1.11.157: There is kamma that is neither dark nor bright with neither dark nor bright result, leading to the ending of kamma.
dn33:1.11.159: previous lives, to be realized through mindfulness;
dn33:1.11.160: the falling away & reappearing of beings, to be realized through the [divine] eye;
dn33:1.11.161: the eight emancipations, to be realized through the body;
dn33:1.11.162: the ending of effluents, to be realized through discernment.
dn33:1.11.164: the flood of sensuality, the flood of becoming, the flood of views, the flood of ignorance.
dn33:1.11.166: the yoke of sensuality, the yoke of becoming, the yoke of views, the yoke of ignorance.
dn33:1.11.168: the unyoking of the yoke of sensuality, the unyoking of the yoke of becoming, the unyoking of the yoke of views, the unyoking of the yoke of ignorance.
dn33:1.11.170: the bodily tie of greed, the bodily tie of ill-will, the bodily tie of habits & precepts, the bodily tie of insistence on ‘This is the truth.’
dn33:1.11.172: clinging to sensuality, clinging to views, clinging to habits & precepts, clinging to doctrines of the self.42
dn33:1.11.174: egg-born generation, placenta-born generation, slime-born generation, spontaneous generation.43
dn33:1.11.176: There is the case, friends, where a certain person descends into the mother’s womb unalert, stays in the mother’s womb unalert, & leaves the mother’s womb unalert. This is the first descent of the embryo.
dn33:1.11.177: There is the case where a certain person descends into the mother’s womb alert, stays in the mother’s womb unalert, & leaves the mother’s womb unalert. This is the second descent of the embryo.
dn33:1.11.178: There is the case where a certain person descends into the mother’s womb alert, stays in the mother’s womb alert, & leaves the mother’s womb unalert. This is the third descent of the embryo.
dn33:1.11.179: There is the case where a certain person descends into the mother’s womb alert, stays in the mother’s womb alert, & leaves the mother’s womb alert. This is the fourth descent of the embryo.
dn33:1.11.181: Friends, there is the acquisition of a self-state where one goes to a self-state through one’s own intention & not through the intention of another.
dn33:1.11.182: There is the acquisition of a self-state where one goes to a self-state through the intention of another & not through one’s own intention.
dn33:1.11.183: There is the acquisition of a self-state where one goes to a self-state both through one’s own intention & through the intention of another.
dn33:1.11.184: There is the acquisition of a self-state where one goes to a self-state neither through one’s own intention nor through the intention of another.
dn33:1.11.186: Friends, there is the offering purified by the donor but not by the recipient.
dn33:1.11.187: There is the offering purified by the recipient but not by the donor.
dn33:1.11.188: There is the offering purified neither by the donor nor by the recipient.
dn33:1.11.189: There is the offering purified both by the donor & by the recipient.
dn33:1.11.190: “Four grounds for the bonds of fellowship:
dn33:1.11.191: giving, kind words, beneficial help, consistency.
dn33:1.11.193: telling lies, divisive speech, harsh speech, idle chatter.
dn33:1.11.195: abstaining from telling lies, abstaining from divisive speech, abstaining from harsh speech, abstaining from idle chatter.
dn33:1.11.197: saying that what has not been seen has been seen, saying that what has not been heard has been heard, saying that what has not been sensed has been sensed, saying that what has not been cognized has been cognized.
dn33:1.11.199: saying that what has not been seen has not been seen, saying that what has not been heard has not been heard, saying that what has not been sensed has not been sensed, saying that what has not been cognized has not been cognized.
dn33:1.11.201: saying that what has been seen has not been seen, saying that what has been heard has not been heard, saying that what has been sensed has not been sensed, saying that what has been cognized has not been cognized.
dn33:1.11.203: saying that what has been seen has been seen, saying that what has been heard has been heard, saying that what has been sensed has been sensed, saying that what has been cognized has been cognized.
dn33:1.11.205: There is the case, friends, where a certain person torments himself and is devoted to the practice of torturing himself.
dn33:1.11.206: There is the case where a certain person torments others and is devoted to the practice of torturing others.
dn33:1.11.207: There is the case where a certain person torments himself and is devoted to the practice of torturing himself, and also torments others and is devoted to the practice of torturing others.
dn33:1.11.208: There is the case where a certain person neither torments himself nor is devoted to the practice of torturing himself, neither torments others nor is devoted to the practice of torturing others.
dn33:1.11.209: Neither tormenting himself nor tormenting others, he dwells in the here-&-now free of hunger, unbound, cooled, sensitive to pleasure, with a Brahmā-like mind.45
dn33:1.11.211: There is the case, friends, where a certain person practices for his/her own benefit but not for the benefit of others.
dn33:1.11.212: There is the case where a certain person practices for the benefit of others but not for his/her own benefit.
dn33:1.11.213: There is the case where a certain person practices neither for his/her own benefit nor for the benefit of others.
dn33:1.11.214: There is the case where a certain person practices both for his/her own benefit & for the benefit of others.46
dn33:1.11.216: one in darkness who is headed for darkness, one in darkness who is headed for light, one in light who is headed for darkness, and one in light who is headed for light.
dn33:1.11.218: the unshakeable contemplative, the white-lotus contemplative, the red-lotus contemplative, the most refined contemplative among contemplatives.
dn33:1.11.219: “These, friends, are the four dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:1.11.220: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:2.1.1: “There are, friends, five dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:2.1.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:2.1.5: the form aggregate, the feeling aggregate, the perception aggregate, the fabrication aggregate, the consciousness aggregate.
dn33:2.1.7: the form clinging-aggregate, the feeling clinging-aggregate, the perception clinging-aggregate, the fabrication clinging-aggregate, the consciousness clinging-aggregate.49
dn33:2.1.9: forms cognizable via the eye—agreeable, pleasing, charming, endearing, enticing, linked with sensual desire;
dn33:2.1.13: tactile sensations cognizable via the body—agreeable, pleasing, charming, endearing, enticing, linked with sensual desire.
dn33:2.1.15: hell, the animal womb, the realm of hungry ghosts, human beings, devas.
dn33:2.1.17: stinginess as to one’s monastery (lodgings), stinginess as to one’s family (of supporters), stinginess as to one’s gains, stinginess as to one’s status, and stinginess as to the Dhamma.50
dn33:2.1.19: the hindrance of sensual desire, the hindrance of ill will, the hindrance of sloth & drowsiness, the hindrance of restlessness & anxiety, the hindrance of uncertainty.
dn33:2.1.21: self-identification views, uncertainty, grasping at habits & practices, sensual desire, ill will.
dn33:2.1.23: passion for form, passion for what is formless, conceit, restlessness, ignorance.
dn33:2.1.25: refraining from taking life, refraining from taking what is not given, refraining from sexual misconduct, refraining from telling lies, refraining from distilled & fermented drinks that cause heedlessness.
dn33:2.1.27: It is impossible, friends, for a monk whose effluents are ended to intentionally deprive a living being of life. It is impossible for a monk whose effluents are ended to take, in the manner of stealing, what is not given. It is impossible for a monk whose effluents are ended to engage in sexual intercourse. It is impossible for a monk whose effluents are ended to tell a conscious lie. It is impossible for a monk whose effluents are ended to consume stored-up sensual things as he did before, when he was a householder.
dn33:2.1.29: loss of relatives, loss of wealth, loss through disease, loss in terms of virtue, loss in terms of views.
dn33:2.1.30: Friends, it’s not by reason of loss of relatives, loss of wealth, or loss through disease that beings—with the break-up of the body, after death—reappear in a plane of deprivation, a bad destination, a lower realm, hell.
dn33:2.1.31: It’s by reason of loss in terms of virtue and loss in terms of views that beings—with the break-up of the body, after death—reappear in a plane of deprivation, a bad destination, a lower realm, hell.
dn33:2.1.33: being consummate in terms of relatives, being consummate in terms of wealth, being consummate in terms of freedom from disease, being consummate in terms of virtue, being consummate in terms of views.
dn33:2.1.34: Friends, it’s not by reason of being consummate in terms of relatives, being consummate in terms of wealth, or being consummate in terms of freedom from disease that beings—with the break-up of the body, after death—reappear in a good destination, a heavenly world.
dn33:2.1.35: It’s by reason of being consummate in virtue and being consummate in views that beings—with the break-up of the body, after death—reappear in a good destination, a heavenly world.
dn33:2.1.36: “Five drawbacks coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue:
dn33:2.1.37: There is the case, friends, where an unvirtuous person, defective in virtue, by reason of heedlessness undergoes the loss/confiscation of great wealth.
dn33:2.1.38: This is the first drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn33:2.1.39: “And further, the bad reputation of the unvirtuous person, defective in virtue, gets spread about.
dn33:2.1.40: This is the second drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn33:2.1.41: “And further, whatever assembly the unvirtuous person, defective in virtue, approaches—whether of noble warriors, brahmans, householders, or contemplatives—he/she does so abashed & without confidence.
dn33:2.1.42: This is the third drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn33:2.1.43: “And further, the unvirtuous person, defective in virtue, dies confused.
dn33:2.1.44: This is the fourth drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn33:2.1.45: “And further, the unvirtuous person, defective in virtue—on the break-up of the body, after death—reappears in a plane of deprivation, a bad destination, a lower realm, hell.
dn33:2.1.46: This is the fifth drawback coming from an unvirtuous person’s defect in virtue.
dn33:2.1.47: “Five rewards coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue:
dn33:2.1.48: There is the case, friends, where a virtuous person, consummate in virtue, by reason of heedfulness acquires a great mass of wealth.
dn33:2.1.49: This is the first reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn33:2.1.50: “And further, the fine reputation of the virtuous person, consummate in virtue, gets spread about.
dn33:2.1.51: This is the second reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn33:2.1.52: “And further, whatever assembly the virtuous person, consummate in virtue, approaches—whether of noble warriors, brahmans, householders, or contemplatives—he/she does so unabashed & with confidence .
dn33:2.1.53: This is the third reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn33:2.1.54: “And further, the virtuous person, consummate in virtue, dies unconfused.
dn33:2.1.55: This is the fourth reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn33:2.1.56: “And further, the virtuous person, consummate in virtue—on the break-up of the body, after death—reappears in a good destination, a heavenly world.
dn33:2.1.57: This is the fifth reward coming from a virtuous person’s consummation in virtue.
dn33:2.1.58: “Friends, a monk making an accusation, when wanting to accuse another, may accuse the other after having established five qualities within himself:
dn33:2.1.59: ‘I will speak at the right time, not at the wrong time.
dn33:2.1.60: I will say what is factual, not what is not factual.
dn33:2.1.61: I will speak gently, and not harshly.
dn33:2.1.62: I will say what is connected with the goal [or:
dn33:2.1.63: the matter at hand], not what is unconnected to the goal [or: the matter at hand].
dn33:2.1.64: I will speak with an attitude of goodwill, and not with inner aversion.’ A monk making an accusation, wanting to accuse another, may accuse the other after having established these five qualities within himself.
dn33:2.1.66: There is the case, friends, where a monk has conviction, is convinced of the Tathāgata’s awakening:
dn33:2.1.67: ‘Indeed, the Blessed One is worthy & rightly self-awakened, consummate in clear-knowing & conduct, well-gone, an expert with regard to the cosmos, unexcelled trainer of people fit to be tamed, teacher of devas & human beings, awakened, blessed.’
dn33:2.1.68: “He is free from illness & discomfort, endowed with good digestion—not too cold, not too hot, of moderate strength—fit for exertion.
dn33:2.1.69: “He is neither fraudulent nor deceitful. He declares himself to the Teacher or to his observant companions in the holy life in line with what he actually is.
dn33:2.1.70: “He keeps his persistence aroused for abandoning unskillful qualities & taking on skillful qualities. He is steadfast, solid in his effort, not shirking his duties with regard to skillful qualities.
dn33:2.1.71: “He is discerning, endowed with discernment leading to the arising of the goal—noble, penetrating, leading to the right ending of stress.
dn33:2.1.73: Aviha (Not Falling Away), Atappa (Untroubled), Sudassa (Good-looking), Sudassī (Clear-seeing), Akaniṭṭha (Peerless).
dn33:2.1.75: one unbound in between, one unbound on arrival, one unbound without fabrication (of exertion), one unbound with fabrication (of exertion), one going upstream to the Peerless.
dn33:2.1.77: There is the case, friends, where a monk is doubtful, uncertain, indecisive about the Teacher and is not confident in him.
dn33:2.1.78: When a monk is doubtful, uncertain, indecisive about the Teacher and is not confident in him, then his mind doesn’t tend toward ardency, commitment, perseverance, or exertion. When one’s mind doesn’t tend toward ardency, commitment, perseverance, or exertion, that is the first mental blockage.
dn33:2.1.79: “There is the case where a monk is doubtful, uncertain, indecisive about the Dhamma…
dn33:2.1.82: “There is the case where a monk is angered & unhappy with his fellows in the holy life, his mind rigid & hostile.
dn33:2.1.83: When a monk is angered & unhappy with his fellows in the holy life, his mind rigid & hostile, then his mind doesn’t tend toward ardency, commitment, perseverance, or exertion. When one’s mind doesn’t tend toward ardency, commitment, perseverance, or exertion, that is the fifth mental blockage.
dn33:2.1.85: There is the case, friends, where a monk is not free of passion, desire, love, thirst, fever, or craving for sensual pleasures.
dn33:2.1.86: When a monk is not free of passion, desire, love, thirst, fever, or craving for sensual pleasures, then his mind doesn’t tend toward ardency, commitment, perseverance, or exertion.
dn33:2.1.87: When one’s mind doesn’t tend toward ardency, commitment, perseverance, or exertion,
dn33:2.1.88: that is the first bondage of awareness.
dn33:2.1.89: “And further, there is the case where a monk is not free of passion, desire, love, thirst, fever, or craving for the body…
dn33:2.1.91: “And further, there is the case where a monk, having eaten as much as his belly will hold, remains committed to the pleasure of lying down, the pleasure of contact, the pleasure of drowsiness…
dn33:2.1.92: “And further, there is the case where a monk practices the holy life intent on being born in one or another of the deva hosts, (thinking,) ‘By this virtue or practice or abstinence or holy life I will be a deva of one sort or another.’
dn33:2.1.93: When a monk practices the holy life intent on being born in one or another of the deva hosts, (thinking,) ‘By this virtue or practice or abstinence or holy life I will be a deva of one sort or another,’ then his mind doesn’t tend toward ardency, commitment, perseverance, or exertion. When one’s mind doesn’t tend toward ardency, commitment, perseverance, or exertion,
dn33:2.1.94: that is the fifth bondage of awareness.
dn33:2.1.96: the faculty of the eye, the faculty of the ear, the faculty of the nose, the faculty of the tongue, the faculty of the body.
dn33:2.1.98: the faculty of pleasure, the faculty of pain, the faculty of happiness, the faculty of distress, the faculty of equanimity.52
dn33:2.1.100: the faculty of conviction, the faculty of persistence, the faculty of mindfulness, the faculty of concentration, the faculty of discernment.53
dn33:2.1.102: There is the case, friends, where the mind of a monk, when attending to sensuality, doesn’t leap up at sensuality, doesn’t grow confident, steadfast, or released in sensuality.
dn33:2.1.103: But when attending to renunciation, his mind leaps up at renunciation, grows confident, steadfast, & released in renunciation.
dn33:2.1.104: When his mind is rightly-gone, rightly developed, has rightly risen above, gained release, & become disjoined from sensuality,
dn33:2.1.105: then whatever effluents, torments, & fevers there are that arise in dependence on sensuality, he is released from them. He doesn’t experience that feeling.
dn33:2.1.106: This is expounded as the escape from sensuality.
dn33:2.1.107: “And further, there is the case where the mind of a monk, when attending to ill will, doesn’t leap up at ill will, doesn’t grow confident, steadfast, or released in ill will.
dn33:2.1.108: But when attending to non-ill will, his mind leaps up at non-ill will, grows confident, steadfast, & released in non-ill will.
dn33:2.1.109: When his mind is rightly-gone, rightly developed, has rightly risen above, gained release, & become disjoined from ill will,
dn33:2.1.110: then whatever effluents, torments, & fevers there are that arise in dependence on ill will, he is released from them. He doesn’t experience that feeling.
dn33:2.1.111: This is expounded as the escape from ill will.
dn33:2.1.112: “And further, there is the case where the mind of a monk, when attending to harmfulness, doesn’t leap up at harmfulness, doesn’t grow confident, steadfast, or released in harmfulness.
dn33:2.1.113: But when attending to harmlessness, his mind leaps up at harmlessness, grows confident, steadfast, & released in harmlessness.
dn33:2.1.114: When his mind is rightly-gone, rightly developed, has rightly risen above, gained release, & become disjoined from harmfulness,
dn33:2.1.115: then whatever effluents, torments, & fevers there are that arise in dependence on harmfulness, he is released from them. He doesn’t experience that feeling.
dn33:2.1.116: This is expounded as the escape from harmfulness.
dn33:2.1.117: “And further, there is the case where the mind of a monk, when attending to forms, doesn’t leap up at forms, doesn’t grow confident, steadfast, or released in forms.
dn33:2.1.118: But when attending to the formless, his mind leaps up at the formless, grows confident, steadfast, & released in the formless.
dn33:2.1.119: When his mind is rightly-gone, rightly developed, has rightly risen above, gained release, & become disjoined from forms,
dn33:2.1.120: then whatever effluents, torments, & fevers there are that arise in dependence on forms, he is released from them. He doesn’t experience that feeling.
dn33:2.1.121: This is expounded as the escape from forms.
dn33:2.1.122: “And further, there is the case where the mind of a monk, when attending to self-identification, doesn’t leap up at self-identification, doesn’t grow confident, steadfast, or released in self-identification.
dn33:2.1.123: But when attending to the cessation of self-identification, his mind leaps up at the cessation of self-identification, grows confident, steadfast, & released in the cessation of self-identification.
dn33:2.1.124: When his mind is rightly-gone, rightly developed, has rightly risen above, gained release, & become disjoined from self-identification,
dn33:2.1.125: then whatever effluents, torments, & fevers there are that arise in dependence on self-identification, he is released from them. He doesn’t experience that feeling.
dn33:2.1.126: This is expounded as the escape from self-identification.
dn33:2.1.128: “There is the case, friends, where the Teacher or a fellow person leading the holy life teaches the Dhamma to a monk.
dn33:2.1.129: And in whatever way the Teacher or a fellow person leading the holy life teaches the Dhamma to the monk, in just that way the monk, with regard to that Dhamma, is sensitive to the meaning, is sensitive to the Dhamma.
dn33:2.1.130: In him—sensitive to the meaning, sensitive to the Dhamma—joy is born. When he is joyful, rapture is born. In one who is enraptured at heart, the body grows calm. When the body is calm, one feels pleasure. Feeling pleasure, the mind becomes concentrated.
dn33:2.1.131: This is the first opening to release.
dn33:2.1.132: “And further, it’s not that the Teacher or a fellow person leading the holy life teaches the Dhamma to the monk. But the monk teaches the Dhamma to others in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it. And in whatever way he teaches the Dhamma to others in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it, in just that way the monk, with regard to that Dhamma, is sensitive to the meaning, is sensitive to the Dhamma. In him—sensitive to the meaning, sensitive to the Dhamma—joy is born. When he is joyful, rapture is born. In one who is enraptured at heart, the body grows calm. When the body is calm, one feels pleasure. Feeling pleasure, the mind becomes concentrated. This is the second opening to release.
dn33:2.1.133: “And further, it’s not that the Teacher or a fellow person leading the holy life teaches the Dhamma to the monk, nor does the monk teach the Dhamma to others in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it. But he recites the Dhamma in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it. In whatever way he recites the Dhamma in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it, in just that way the monk, with regard to that Dhamma, is sensitive to the meaning, is sensitive to the Dhamma. In him—sensitive to the meaning, sensitive to the Dhamma—joy is born. When he is joyful, rapture is born. In one who is enraptured at heart, the body grows calm. When the body is calm, one feels pleasure. Feeling pleasure, the mind becomes concentrated. This is the third opening to release.
dn33:2.1.134: “And further, it’s not that the Teacher or a fellow person leading the holy life teaches the Dhamma to the monk; nor does the monk teach the Dhamma to others in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it; nor does he recite the Dhamma in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it. But in his awareness he directs his thoughts to the Dhamma in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it; he evaluates it, & mentally examines it. In whatever way he, in his awareness, directs his thoughts to the Dhamma in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it, evaluates it, & mentally examines it, in just that way the monk, with regard to that Dhamma, is sensitive to the meaning, is sensitive to the Dhamma. In him—sensitive to the meaning, sensitive to the Dhamma—joy is born. When he is joyful, rapture is born. In one who is enraptured at heart, the body grows calm. When the body is calm, one feels pleasure. Feeling pleasure, the mind becomes concentrated. This is the fourth opening to release.
dn33:2.1.135: “And further, it’s not that the Teacher or a fellow person leading the holy life teaches the Dhamma to the monk; nor does the monk teach the Dhamma to others in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it; nor does he recite the Dhamma in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it; nor does he, in his awareness, direct his thoughts to the Dhamma in detail, as he has heard it, as he has learned it, evaluate it, or mentally examine it. But he has well grasped a certain theme of concentration, has attended to it well, sustained it well, has penetrated it well with discernment.
dn33:2.1.136: In whatever way he has well grasped a certain theme of concentration, has attended to it well, sustained it well, has penetrated it well with discernment, in just that way the monk, with regard to that Dhamma, is sensitive to the meaning, is sensitive to the Dhamma.
dn33:2.1.137: In him—sensitive to the meaning, sensitive to the Dhamma—joy is born. When he is joyful, rapture is born. In one who is enraptured at heart, the body grows calm. When the body is calm, one feels pleasure. Feeling pleasure, the mind becomes concentrated.
dn33:2.1.138: This is the fifth opening to release.
dn33:2.1.139: “Five perceptions for the maturing of release:
dn33:2.1.140: the perception of inconstancy, the perception of stress in what is inconstant, the perception of not-self in what is stressful, the perception of abandoning, the perception of dispassion.54
dn33:2.1.141: “These, friends, are the five dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:2.1.142: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:2.2.1: “There are, friends, six dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:2.2.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:2.2.5: the eye as a sense medium, the ear as a sense medium, the nose as a sense medium, the tongue as a sense medium, the body as a sense medium, the intellect as a sense medium.
dn33:2.2.7: form as a sense medium, sound as a sense medium, aroma as a sense medium, flavor as a sense medium, tactile sensation as a sense medium, idea as a sense medium.
dn33:2.2.9: eye-consciousness, ear-consciousness, nose-consciousness, tongue-consciousness, body-consciousness, intellect-consciousness.
dn33:2.2.11: eye-contact, ear-contact, nose-contact, tongue-contact, body-contact, intellect-contact.
dn33:2.2.13: feeling born of eye-contact, feeling born of ear-contact, feeling born of nose-contact, feeling born of tongue-contact, feeling born of body-contact, feeling born of intellect-contact.
dn33:2.2.15: perception of forms, perception of sounds, perception of aromas, perception of flavors, perception of tactile sensations, perception of ideas.
dn33:2.2.17: intention for forms, intention for sounds, intention for aromas, intention for flavors, intention for tactile sensations, intention for ideas.
dn33:2.2.19: craving for forms, craving for sounds, craving for aromas, craving for flavors, craving for tactile sensations, craving for ideas.
dn33:2.2.21: There is the case, friends, where a monk dwells without respect or deference for the Teacher… for the Dhamma… for the Saṅgha… for the training… for heedfulness… for welcoming manners.
dn33:2.2.23: There is the case, friends, where a monk dwells with respect & deference for the Teacher… for the Dhamma… for the Saṅgha… for the training… for heedfulness… for welcoming manners.
dn33:2.2.25: On seeing a form via the eye, one examines a form that can act as the basis for happiness.
dn33:2.2.26: On hearing a sound via the ear…
dn33:2.2.27: On smelling an aroma via the nose…
dn33:2.2.28: On tasting a flavor via the tongue…
dn33:2.2.29: On sensing a tactile sensation via the body…
dn33:2.2.30: On cognizing an idea via the intellect, one examines an idea that can act as the basis for happiness.
dn33:2.2.32: On seeing a form via the eye, one examines a form that can act as the basis for distress. On hearing a sound via the ear… On smelling an aroma via the nose… On tasting a flavor via the tongue… On sensing a tactile sensation via the body…
dn33:2.2.33: On cognizing an idea via the intellect, one examines an idea that can act as the basis for distress.
dn33:2.2.35: On seeing a form via the eye, one examines a form that can act as the basis for equanimity. On hearing a sound via the ear… On smelling an aroma via the nose… On tasting a flavor via the tongue… On sensing a tactile sensation via the body…
dn33:2.2.36: On cognizing an idea via the intellect, one examines an idea that can act as the basis for equanimity.
dn33:2.2.38: There is the case, friends, where a monk is set on bodily acts of goodwill with regard to his companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs.
dn33:2.2.39: This is a condition that is conducive to amiability, that engenders feelings of endearment, engenders feelings of respect, leading to a sense of fellowship, a lack of disputes, harmony, & a state of unity.
dn33:2.2.40: “And further, the monk is set on verbal acts of goodwill with regard to his companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs.
dn33:2.2.41: This, too, is a condition that is conducive to amiability, that engenders feelings of endearment, engenders feelings of respect, leading to a sense of fellowship, a lack of disputes, harmony, & a state of unity.
dn33:2.2.42: “And further, the monk is set on mental acts of goodwill with regard to his companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs.
dn33:2.2.43: This, too, is a condition that is conducive to amiability, that engenders feelings of endearment, engenders feelings of respect, leading to a sense of fellowship, a lack of disputes, harmony, & a state of unity.
dn33:2.2.44: “And further, whatever righteous gains the monk may obtain in a righteous way—even if only the alms in his bowl—he does not consume them alone. He consumes them after sharing them in common with his virtuous companions in the holy life.
dn33:2.2.45: This, too, is a condition that is conducive to amiability, that engenders feelings of endearment, engenders feelings of respect, leading to a sense of fellowship, a lack of disputes, harmony, & a state of unity.
dn33:2.2.46: “And further—with reference to the virtues that are untorn, unbroken, unspotted, unsplattered, liberating, praised by the observant, ungrasped at, leading to concentration—the monk dwells with his virtue in tune with that of his companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs.
dn33:2.2.47: This, too, is a condition that is conducive to amiability, that engenders feelings of endearment, engenders feelings of respect, leading to a sense of fellowship, a lack of disputes, harmony, & a state of unity.
dn33:2.2.48: “And further—with reference to the view that is noble, leading outward, that leads those who act in accordance with it to the right ending of suffering & stress—the monk dwells with his view in tune with that of his companions in the holy life, to their faces & behind their backs.
dn33:2.2.49: This, too, is a condition that is conducive to amiability, that engenders feelings of endearment, engenders feelings of respect, leading to a sense of fellowship, a lack of disputes, harmony, & a state of unity.
dn33:2.2.51: There is the case, friends, where a monk is angry & spiteful.
dn33:2.2.52: When a monk is angry & spiteful, he dwells without respect & without deference for the Teacher, for the Dhamma, for the Saṅgha, and does not fulfill the training.
dn33:2.2.53: He gives rise to a dispute in the Saṅgha. That dispute will be for the harm & suffering of many, for the detriment, harm, & suffering of devas & human beings.
dn33:2.2.54: If you see this sort of root of dispute within you or without, friends, you should make an effort for the abandoning of that evil root of dispute.
dn33:2.2.55: If you don’t see this sort of root of dispute within you or without, you should practice for the future non-arising of that evil root of dispute.
dn33:2.2.56: Thus is its abandoning, thus its future non-arising.
dn33:2.2.57: “And further, there is the case where a monk is dismissive & antagonistic…
dn33:2.2.62: When a monk is opinionated, tenacious, & obstinate, he dwells without respect & without deference for the Teacher, for the Dhamma, for the Saṅgha, and does not fulfill the training.
dn33:2.2.63: He gives rise to a dispute in the Saṅgha. That dispute will be for the harm & suffering of many, for the detriment, harm, & suffering of devas & human beings.
dn33:2.2.64: If you see this sort of root of dispute within you or without, friends, you should make an effort for the abandoning of that evil root of dispute.
dn33:2.2.65: If you don’t see this sort of root of dispute within you or without, you should practice for the future non-arising of that evil root of dispute.
dn33:2.2.66: Thus is its abandoning, thus its future non-arising.
dn33:2.2.68: the earth property, the liquid property, the fire property, the wind property, the space property, the consciousness property.55
dn33:2.2.69: “Six properties that are means of escape:
dn33:2.2.70: There is the case, friends, where a monk might say,
dn33:2.2.71: ‘Although goodwill has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken by me as my awareness-release,
dn33:2.2.72: still ill will keeps overpowering my mind.’ He should be told, ‘Don’t say that.
dn33:2.2.73: You shouldn’t speak in that way. Don’t misrepresent the Blessed One, for it’s not right to misrepresent the Blessed One, and the Blessed One wouldn’t say that.
dn33:2.2.74: It’s impossible, there is no way that—when goodwill has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken
dn33:2.2.75: as an awareness-release—ill will would still keep overpowering the mind. That possibility doesn’t exist,
dn33:2.2.76: for this is the escape from ill will: goodwill as an awareness-release.’
dn33:2.2.77: “And further, there is the case where a monk might say,
dn33:2.2.78: ‘Although compassion has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken by me as my awareness-release,
dn33:2.2.79: still harmfulness keeps overpowering my mind.’ He should be told, ‘Don’t say that.
dn33:2.2.80: You shouldn’t speak in that way. Don’t misrepresent the Blessed One, for it’s not right to misrepresent the Blessed One, and the Blessed One wouldn’t say that.
dn33:2.2.81: It’s impossible, there is no way that—when compassion has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken as an awareness-release—
dn33:2.2.82: harmfulness would still keep overpowering the mind. That possibility doesn’t exist,
dn33:2.2.83: for this is the escape from harmfulness: compassion as an awareness-release.’
dn33:2.2.84: “And further, there is the case where a monk might say,
dn33:2.2.85: ‘Although empathetic joy has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken by me as my awareness-release,
dn33:2.2.86: still resentment keeps overpowering my mind.’
dn33:2.2.87: He should be told, ‘Don’t say that. You shouldn’t speak in that way. Don’t misrepresent the Blessed One, for it’s not right to misrepresent the Blessed One, and the Blessed One wouldn’t say that.
dn33:2.2.88: It’s impossible, there is no way that—when empathetic joy has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken as an awareness-release—
dn33:2.2.89: resentment would still keep overpowering the mind. That possibility doesn’t exist,
dn33:2.2.90: for this is the escape from resentment: empathetic joy as an awareness-release.’
dn33:2.2.91: “And further, there is the case where a monk might say,
dn33:2.2.92: ‘Although equanimity has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken by me as my awareness-release,
dn33:2.2.93: still passion keeps overpowering my mind.’ He should be told, ‘Don’t say that.
dn33:2.2.94: You shouldn’t speak in that way. Don’t misrepresent the Blessed One, for it’s not right to misrepresent the Blessed One, and the Blessed One wouldn’t say that.
dn33:2.2.95: It’s impossible, there is no way that—when equanimity has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken as an awareness-release—
dn33:2.2.96: passion would still keep overpowering the mind. That possibility doesn’t exist,
dn33:2.2.97: for this is the escape from passion: equanimity as an awareness-release.’
dn33:2.2.98: “And further, there is the case where a monk might say,
dn33:2.2.99: ‘Although the signless has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken by me as my awareness-release,
dn33:2.2.100: still my consciousness follows the drift of signs.’
dn33:2.2.101: He should be told, ‘Don’t say that. You shouldn’t speak in that way. Don’t misrepresent the Blessed One, for it’s not right to misrepresent the Blessed One, and the Blessed One wouldn’t say that.
dn33:2.2.102: It’s impossible, there is no way that—when the signless has been developed, pursued, handed the reins, taken as a basis, steadied, consolidated, & well-undertaken as an awareness-release—
dn33:2.2.103: consciousness would follow the drift of signs. That possibility doesn’t exist,
dn33:2.2.104: for this is the escape from all signs: the signless as an awareness-release.’
dn33:2.2.105: “And further, there is the case where a monk might say,
dn33:2.2.106: ‘Although “I am” is gone, and I do not assume that “I am this,”
dn33:2.2.107: still the arrow of uncertainty & perplexity keeps overpowering my mind.’
dn33:2.2.108: He should be told, ‘Don’t say that. You shouldn’t speak in that way. Don’t misrepresent the Blessed One, for it’s not right to misrepresent the Blessed One, and the Blessed One wouldn’t say that.
dn33:2.2.109: It’s impossible, there is no way that—when “I am” is gone, and “I am this” is not assumed—
dn33:2.2.110: the arrow of uncertainty & perplexity would keep overpowering the mind.
dn33:2.2.111: That possibility doesn’t exist, for this is the escape from the arrow of uncertainty & perplexity: the uprooting of the conceit, “I am”.’
dn33:2.2.113: unsurpassed vision, unsurpassed hearing, unsurpassed gains, unsurpassed trainings, unsurpassed service, unsurpassed objects of recollection.
dn33:2.2.115: recollection of the Buddha, recollection of the Dhamma, recollection of the Saṅgha, recollection of virtue, recollection of generosity, recollection of the devas.56
dn33:2.2.117: There is the case, friends, where a monk, on seeing a form via the eye, is neither gladdened nor saddened, but remains equanimous, mindful, & alert.
dn33:2.2.118: On hearing a sound via the ear… On smelling an aroma via the nose… On tasting a flavor via the tongue… On sensing a tactile sensation via the body…
dn33:2.2.119: On cognizing an idea via the intellect, he is neither gladdened nor saddened, but remains equanimous, mindful, & alert.
dn33:2.2.121: There is the case, friends, where one born in darkness gives birth to dark qualities.
dn33:2.2.122: There is the case where one born in darkness gives birth to bright qualities.
dn33:2.2.123: There is the case where one born in darkness gives birth to unbinding, neither dark nor bright.
dn33:2.2.124: There is the case where one born in brightness gives birth to bright qualities.
dn33:2.2.125: There is the case where one born in brightness gives birth to dark qualities.
dn33:2.2.126: There is the case where one born in brightness gives birth to unbinding, neither dark nor bright.
dn33:2.2.128: the perception of inconstancy, the perception of stress in what is inconstant, the perception of not-self in what is stressful, the perception of abandoning, the perception of dispassion, the perception of cessation.
dn33:2.2.129: “These, friends, are the six dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:2.2.130: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:2.3.1: “There are, friends, seven dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:2.3.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:2.3.5: the treasure of conviction, the treasure of virtue, the treasure of a sense of shame, the treasure of a sense of compunction, the treasure of listening, the treasure of generosity, the treasure of discernment.58
dn33:2.3.7: mindfulness as a factor for awakening, analysis of qualities as a factor for awakening, persistence as a factor for awakening, rapture as a factor for awakening, calm as a factor for awakening, concentration as a factor for awakening, equanimity as a factor for awakening.59
dn33:2.3.9: right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness.60
dn33:2.3.11: There is the case, friends, where a monk is without conviction, without a sense of shame, without a sense of compunction, without learning, lazy, one of muddled truth, & undiscerning.
dn33:2.3.13: There is the case, friends, where a monk has conviction, a sense of shame, a sense of compunction, learning, and is one of aroused persistence, established mindfulness, & discerning.
dn33:2.3.14: “Seven qualities of a person of integrity:
dn33:2.3.15: There is the case, friends, where a monk is one with a sense of Dhamma, a sense of meaning, a sense of himself, a sense of moderation, a sense of time, a sense of social gatherings, & a sense of distinctions among individuals.62
dn33:2.3.17: There is the case, friends, where a monk has keen desire to undertake the training & has not discarded his love for undertaking the training in the future.
dn33:2.3.18: He has keen desire to attend carefully to the Dhamma…
dn33:2.3.23: to penetrate (right) view & has not discarded his love for penetrating (right) view in the future.
dn33:2.3.25: the perception of inconstancy, the perception of not-self, the perception of unattractiveness, the perception of drawbacks, the perception of abandoning, the perception of dispassion, the perception of cessation.63
