dn33:2.3.27: the strength of conviction, the strength of persistence, the strength of a sense of shame, the strength of compunction, the strength of mindfulness, the strength of concentration, the strength of discernment.64
dn33:2.3.29: Friends, there are beings with multiplicity of body & multiplicity of perception, such as human beings, some devas, & some beings in the lower realms.
dn33:2.3.30: This is the first station of consciousness.
dn33:2.3.31: “There are beings with multiplicity of body & singularity of perception, such as the devas of Brahmā’s retinue generated by the first (jhāna).
dn33:2.3.32: This is the second station of consciousness.
dn33:2.3.33: “There are beings with singularity of body & multiplicity of perception, such as the Radiant Devas.
dn33:2.3.34: This is the third station of consciousness.
dn33:2.3.35: “There are beings with singularity of body & singularity of perception, such as the Beautiful Black Devas.
dn33:2.3.36: This is the fourth station of consciousness.
dn33:2.3.37: “There are beings who, with the complete transcending of perceptions of (physical) form, with the disappearance of perceptions of resistance, and not attending to perceptions of multiplicity, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite space,’ arrive at the dimension of the infinitude of space.
dn33:2.3.38: This is the fifth station of consciousness.
dn33:2.3.39: “There are beings who, with the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of space, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite consciousness,’ arrive at the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness.
dn33:2.3.40: This is the sixth station of consciousness.
dn33:2.3.41: “There are beings who, with the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness, (perceiving,) ‘There is nothing,’ arrive at the dimension of nothingness.
dn33:2.3.42: This is the seventh station of consciousness.
dn33:2.3.44: one (released) both ways, one released through discernment, a bodily witness, one attained to view, one released through conviction, a Dhamma-follower, a conviction-follower.
dn33:2.3.46: the obsession of sensual passion, the obsession of resistance, the obsession of views, the obsession of uncertainty, the obsession of conceit, the obsession of passion for becoming, the obsession of ignorance.
dn33:2.3.48: the fetter of sensuality, the fetter of irritation, the fetter of views, the fetter of uncertainty, the fetter of conceit, the fetter of passion for becoming, the fetter of ignorance.
dn33:2.3.49: “Seven means for the settling of issues: For the settling & stilling of issues that have arisen:
dn33:2.3.50: “A face-to-face verdict should be given. “A verdict of mindfulness may be given. “A verdict of past insanity may be given. “Acting in accordance with what is admitted. “Acting in accordance with the majority. “Acting for his [the accused’s] further punishment. “Covering over as with grass.
dn33:2.3.51: “These, friends, are the seven dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:2.3.52: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:3.1.1: “There are, friends, eight dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:3.1.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:3.1.5: wrong view, wrong resolve, wrong speech, wrong action, wrong livelihood, wrong effort, wrong mindfulness, wrong concentration.
dn33:3.1.7: right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.
dn33:3.1.9: a stream-winner, one practicing for the realization of the fruit of stream-entry, a once-returner, one practicing for the realization of the fruit of once-returning, a non-returner, one practicing for the realization of the fruit of non-returning, an arahant, one practicing for the realization of arahantship.
dn33:3.1.11: There is the case, friends, where a monk has some work to do.
dn33:3.1.13: ‘I will have to do this work. But when I have done this work, my body will be tired. Why don’t I lie down?’
dn33:3.1.14: So he lies down. He doesn’t make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.15: This is the first ground for laziness.
dn33:3.1.16: “Then there is the case where a monk has done some work.
dn33:3.1.18: ‘I have done some work. Now that I have done work, my body is tired. Why don’t I lie down?’
dn33:3.1.19: So he lies down. He doesn’t make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.20: This is the second ground for laziness.
dn33:3.1.21: “Then there is the case where a monk has to go on a journey.
dn33:3.1.23: ‘I will have to go on this journey. But when I have gone on the journey, my body will be tired. Why don’t I lie down?’
dn33:3.1.24: So he lies down. He doesn’t make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.25: This is the third ground for laziness.
dn33:3.1.26: “Then there is the case where a monk has gone on a journey.
dn33:3.1.28: ‘I have gone on a journey. Now that I have gone on a journey, my body is tired. Why don’t I lie down?’
dn33:3.1.29: So he lies down. He doesn’t make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.30: This is the fourth ground for laziness.
dn33:3.1.31: “Then there is the case where a monk, having gone for alms in a village or town, does not get as much coarse or refined food as he would like for his fill.
dn33:3.1.33: ‘I, having gone for alms in a village or town, have not gotten as much coarse or refined food as I would like for my fill. This body of mine is tired & unsuitable for work. Why don’t I lie down?’
dn33:3.1.34: So he lies down. He doesn’t make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.35: This is the fifth ground for laziness.
dn33:3.1.36: “Then there is the case where a monk, having gone for alms in a village or town, gets as much coarse or refined food as he would like for his fill.
dn33:3.1.38: ‘I, having gone for alms in a village or town, have gotten as much coarse or refined food as I would like for my fill. This body of mine is heavy & unsuitable for work—stuffed with beans, as it were. Why don’t I lie down?’
dn33:3.1.39: So he lies down. He doesn’t make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.40: This is the sixth ground for laziness.
dn33:3.1.41: “Then there is the case where a monk comes down with a slight illness.
dn33:3.1.43: ‘I have come down with a slight illness.
dn33:3.1.44: There’s a need to lie down.’
dn33:3.1.45: So he lies down. He doesn’t make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.46: This is the seventh ground for laziness.
dn33:3.1.47: “Then there is the case where a monk has recovered from his illness, not long after his recovery.
dn33:3.1.49: ‘I have recovered from my illness. It’s not long after my recovery. This body of mine is weak & unsuitable for work. Why don’t I lie down?’
dn33:3.1.50: So he lies down. He doesn’t make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.51: This is the eighth ground for laziness.
dn33:3.1.52: “Eight grounds for the arousal of energy:
dn33:3.1.53: There is the case, friends, where a monk has some work to do.
dn33:3.1.55: ‘I will have to do this work. But when I am doing this work, it will not be easy to attend to the Buddha’s message. Why don’t I make an effort beforehand for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized?’
dn33:3.1.56: So he makes an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.57: This is the first ground for the arousal of energy.
dn33:3.1.58: “Then there is the case where a monk has done some work.
dn33:3.1.60: ‘I have done some work. While I was doing work, I couldn’t attend to the Buddha’s message. Why don’t I make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized?’
dn33:3.1.61: So he makes an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.62: This is the second ground for the arousal of energy.
dn33:3.1.63: “Then there is the case where a monk has to go on a journey.
dn33:3.1.65: ‘I will have to go on this journey. But when I am going on the journey, it will not be easy to attend to the Buddha’s message.
dn33:3.1.66: Why don’t I make an effort beforehand for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized?’
dn33:3.1.67: So he makes an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.68: This is the third ground for the arousal of energy.
dn33:3.1.69: “Then there is the case where a monk has gone on a journey.
dn33:3.1.71: ‘I have gone on a journey. While I was going on the journey, I couldn’t attend to the Buddha’s message. Why don’t I make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized?’
dn33:3.1.72: So he makes an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.73: This is the fourth ground for the arousal of energy.
dn33:3.1.74: “Then there is the case where a monk, having gone for alms in a village or town, does not get as much coarse or refined food as he would like for his fill.
dn33:3.1.76: ‘I, having gone for alms in a village or town, have not gotten as much coarse or refined food as I would like for my fill. This body of mine is light & suitable for work. Why don’t I make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized?’
dn33:3.1.77: So he makes an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.78: This is the fifth ground for the arousal of energy.
dn33:3.1.79: “Then there is the case where a monk, having gone for alms in a village or town, gets as much coarse or refined food as he would like for his fill.
dn33:3.1.81: ‘I, having gone for alms in a village or town, have gotten as much coarse or refined food as I would like for my fill. This body of mine is light & suitable for work. Why don’t I make an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized?’
dn33:3.1.82: So he makes an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.83: This is the sixth ground for the arousal of energy.
dn33:3.1.84: “Then there is the case where a monk comes down with a slight illness.
dn33:3.1.86: ‘I have come down with a slight illness. Now, there’s the possibility that it could get worse. Why don’t I make an effort beforehand for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized?’
dn33:3.1.87: So he makes an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.88: This is the seventh ground for the arousal of energy.
dn33:3.1.89: “Then there is the case where a monk has recovered from his illness, not long after his recovery.
dn33:3.1.91: ‘I have recovered from my illness. It’s not long after my recovery. Now, there’s the possibility that the illness could come back. Why don’t I make an effort beforehand for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized?’
dn33:3.1.92: So he makes an effort for the attaining of the as-yet-unattained, the reaching of the as-yet-unreached, the realization of the as-yet-unrealized.
dn33:3.1.93: This is the eighth ground for the arousal of energy.
dn33:3.1.95: One gives a gift on impulse. One gives a gift from fear. One gives a gift (thinking,) ‘He/she gave to me.’ One gives a gift (thinking,) ‘He/she will give to me.’ One gives a gift (thinking,) ‘I am cooking. These are not cooking. It’s not right, when one is cooking, not to give to those who are not cooking.’ One gives a gift (thinking,) ‘When this gift has been given, my admirable reputation will spread about.’
dn33:3.1.96: One gives a gift as an ornament for the mind, as a support for the mind.
dn33:3.1.98: There is the case, friends, where a certain individual gives a gift to a contemplative or a brahman—food, drink, cloth, sandals, garlands, scents, ointments, beds, dwellings, lighting—
dn33:3.1.99: and he expects a return for what he gave.
dn33:3.1.100: He sees a prosperous noble warrior, a prosperous brahman, or a prosperous householder going about endowed & provided with the five strings of sensuality.
dn33:3.1.102: ‘O that—with the breakup of the body, after death—I may be reborn among prosperous noble warriors, prosperous brahmans, or prosperous householders!’
dn33:3.1.103: He fixes on that thought, determines on that thought, develops that thought. And that thought, released on a low level and not developed to anything higher, leads to rebirth right there.
dn33:3.1.104: But I say this of a virtuous person, not of an unvirtuous one.
dn33:3.1.105: A virtuous person’s act of directing awareness succeeds through purity.
dn33:3.1.106: “And further, a certain person gives a gift to a contemplative or a brahman…
dn33:3.1.109: ‘The Devas of the Four Great Kings are long-lived, beautiful, & replete with pleasure.’
dn33:3.1.111: ‘O that—with the breakup of the body, after death—I may be reborn among the Devas of the Four Great Kings!’
dn33:3.1.112: He fixes on that thought, determines on that thought, develops that thought. And that thought, released on a low level and not developed to anything higher, leads to rebirth right there.
dn33:3.1.113: But I say this of a virtuous person, not of an unvirtuous one.
dn33:3.1.114: A virtuous person’s act of directing awareness succeeds through purity.
dn33:3.1.115: “And further, a certain person gives a gift to a contemplative or a brahman…
dn33:3.1.121: the Devas Who Delight in Creation…
dn33:3.1.122: the Devas Who Wield Power Over the Creations of Others are long-lived, beautiful, & replete with pleasure.’
dn33:3.1.124: ‘O that—with the breakup of the body, after death—I may be reborn among the Devas Who Wield Power Over the Creations of Others!’
dn33:3.1.125: He fixes on that thought, determines on that thought, develops that thought. And that thought, released on a low level and not developed to anything higher, leads to rebirth right there.
dn33:3.1.126: But I say this of a virtuous person, not of an unvirtuous one.
dn33:3.1.127: A virtuous person’s act of directing awareness succeeds through purity.
dn33:3.1.128: “And further, a certain person gives a gift to a contemplative or a brahman—food, drink, cloth, sandals, garlands, scents, ointments, beds, dwellings, lighting—
dn33:3.1.129: and he expects a return for what he gave.
dn33:3.1.131: ‘The Devas of Brahmā’s Retinue are long-lived, beautiful, & replete with pleasure.’
dn33:3.1.133: ‘O that—with the breakup of the body, after death—I may be reborn among the Devas of Brahmā’s Retinue!’
dn33:3.1.134: He fixes on that thought, determines on that thought, develops that thought. And that thought, released on a low level and not developed to anything higher, leads to rebirth right there.
dn33:3.1.135: But I say this of a virtuous person, not of an unvirtuous one,
dn33:3.1.136: of one devoid of passion, not of one with passion.
dn33:3.1.137: A virtuous person’s act of directing awareness succeeds through purity.
dn33:3.1.139: a noble warrior assembly, a brahman assembly, a householder assembly, a contemplative assembly, a Four Great Kings assembly, a (Devas of the) Thirty-three assembly, a Māra assembly,69 a Brahmā assembly.
dn33:3.1.141: gain, loss, status, disgrace, censure, praise, pleasure, pain.
dn33:3.1.143: One percipient of form internally sees forms externally as limited, beautiful & ugly. Mastering them, he is percipient of ‘I know; I see.’
dn33:3.1.144: This is the first dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn33:3.1.145: “One percipient of form internally sees forms externally as immeasurable, beautiful & ugly. Mastering them, he is percipient of ‘I know; I see.’
dn33:3.1.147: This is the second dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn33:3.1.148: “One percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as limited, beautiful & ugly. Mastering them, he is percipient of ‘I know; I see.’
dn33:3.1.149: This is the third dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn33:3.1.150: “One percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as immeasurable, beautiful & ugly. Mastering them, he is percipient of ‘I know; I see.’
dn33:3.1.151: This is the fourth dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn33:3.1.152: “One percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as blue, blue in their color, blue in their features, blue in their glow.
dn33:3.1.153: Just as a flax-flower is blue, blue in its color, blue in its features, blue in its glow, or just as Vārāṇasī muslin, smooth on both sides, is blue, blue in its color, blue in its features, blue in its glow,
dn33:3.1.154: in the same way one percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as blue, blue in their color, blue in their features, blue in their glow. Mastering them, he is percipient of ‘I know; I see.’
dn33:3.1.155: This is the fifth dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn33:3.1.156: “One percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as yellow, yellow in their color, yellow in their features, yellow in their glow.
dn33:3.1.157: Just as a kaṇṇikāra-flower is yellow, yellow in its color, yellow in its features, yellow in its glow, or just as Vārāṇasī muslin, smooth on both sides, is yellow, yellow in its color, yellow in its features, yellow in its glow,
dn33:3.1.158: in the same way one percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as yellow, yellow in their color, yellow in their features, yellow in their glow. Mastering them, he is percipient of ‘I know; I see.’
dn33:3.1.159: This is the sixth dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn33:3.1.160: “One percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as red, red in their color, red in their features, red in their glow.
dn33:3.1.161: Just as a bandha-jīvaka-flower is red, red in its color, red in its features, red in its glow, or just as Vārāṇasī muslin, smooth on both sides, is red, red in its color, red in its features, red in its glow,
dn33:3.1.162: in the same way one percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as red, red in their color, red in their features, red in their glow. Mastering them, he is percipient of ‘I know; I see.’
dn33:3.1.163: This is the seventh dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn33:3.1.164: “One percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as white, white in their color, white in their features, white in their glow.
dn33:3.1.165: Just as the morning star is white, white in its color, white in its features, white in its glow, or just as Vārāṇasī muslin, smooth on both sides, is white, white in its color, white in its features, white in its glow,
dn33:3.1.166: in the same way one percipient of the formless internally sees forms externally as white, white in their color, white in their features, white in their glow. Mastering them, he is percipient of ‘I know; I see.’
dn33:3.1.167: This is the eighth dimension of (mental) mastery.
dn33:3.1.169: Possessed of form, one sees forms.
dn33:3.1.171: “Not percipient of form internally, one sees forms externally.
dn33:3.1.173: “One is intent only on the beautiful.
dn33:3.1.175: “With the complete transcending of perceptions of (physical) form, with the disappearance of perceptions of resistance, and not attending to perceptions of multiplicity, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite space,’ one enters & remains in the dimension of the infinitude of space.
dn33:3.1.177: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of space, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite consciousness,’ one enters & remains in the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness.
dn33:3.1.179: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness, (perceiving,) ‘There is nothing,’ one enters & remains in the dimension of nothingness.
dn33:3.1.181: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of nothingness, one enters & remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.
dn33:3.1.183: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, one enters & remains in the cessation of perception and feeling.
dn33:3.1.185: “These, friends, are the eight dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:3.1.186: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:3.2.1: “There are, friends, nine dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:3.2.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:3.2.5: “(Thinking,) ‘He has done me harm,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.6: ‘He is doing me harm,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.7: ‘He is going to do me harm,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.8: ‘He has done harm to people who are dear & pleasing to me,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.9: ‘He is doing harm to people who are dear & pleasing to me,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.10: ‘He is going to do harm to people who are dear & pleasing to me,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.11: ‘He has aided people who are not dear or pleasing to me,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.12: ‘He is aiding people who are not dear or pleasing to me,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.13: ‘He is going to aid people who are not dear or pleasing to me,’ one binds hatred.
dn33:3.2.15: (Thinking,) ‘He has done me harm. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.16: ‘He is doing me harm. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.17: ‘He is going to do me harm. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.18: ‘He has done harm to people who are dear & pleasing to me. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.19: ‘He is doing harm to people who are dear & pleasing to me. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.20: ‘He is going to do harm to people who are dear & pleasing to me. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.21: ‘He has aided people who are not dear or pleasing to me. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.22: ‘He is aiding people who are not dear or pleasing to me. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.23: ‘He is going to aid people who are not dear or pleasing to me. But what should I expect?’ one subdues hatred.
dn33:3.2.25: There are beings with multiplicity of body & multiplicity of perception, such as human beings, some devas, and some beings in the lower realms.
dn33:3.2.26: This is the first abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.27: “There are beings with multiplicity of body & singularity of perception, such as the devas of Brahmā’s retinue generated by the first (jhāna).
dn33:3.2.28: This is the second abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.29: “There are beings with singularity of body & multiplicity of perception, such as the Radiant Devas.
dn33:3.2.30: This is the third abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.31: “There are beings with singularity of body & singularity of perception, such as the Beautiful Black Devas.
dn33:3.2.32: This is the fourth abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.33: “There are beings who are non-percipient, insensitive, such as the Non-percipient-being Devas.
dn33:3.2.34: This is the fifth abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.35: “There are beings who, with the complete transcending of perceptions of (physical) form, with the disappearance of perceptions of resistance, and not attending to perceptions of multiplicity, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite space,’ arrive at the dimension of the infinitude of space.
dn33:3.2.36: This is the sixth abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.37: “There are beings who, with the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of space, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite consciousness,’ arrive at the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness.
dn33:3.2.38: This is the seventh abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.39: “There are beings who, with the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness, (perceiving,) ‘There is nothing,’ arrive at the dimension of nothingness.
dn33:3.2.40: This is the eighth abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.41: “There are beings who, with the complete transcending of the dimension of nothingness, arrive at the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.
dn33:3.2.42: This is the ninth abode for beings.
dn33:3.2.43: “Nine inopportune, untimely situations for leading the holy life:
dn33:3.2.44: There is the case, friends, where a Tathāgata appears in the world, worthy & rightly self-awakened. The Dhamma is taught that leads to stilling, to total unbinding, to self-awakening as declared by One Well-gone.
dn33:3.2.45: But this person is reborn in hell.
dn33:3.2.46: This is the first inopportune, untimely situation for leading the holy life.
dn33:3.2.47: “And further, there is the case where a Tathāgata appears in the world…
dn33:3.2.48: but this person is reborn in an animal womb…
dn33:3.2.51: in the realm of the hungry ghosts…
dn33:3.2.54: in the host of the asuras…
dn33:3.2.57: in a certain host of long-lived devas…
dn33:3.2.60: or he is born in a border region among ignorant barbarians where there is no opening for monks, nuns, male lay followers, or female lay followers to go.…
dn33:3.2.63: “Or he is born in the Middle Country [the middle Ganges Valley] but he has wrong views & skewed vision:
dn33:3.2.64: ‘There is nothing given, nothing offered, nothing sacrificed. There is no fruit or result of good or bad actions. There is no this world, no next world, no mother, no father; there are no spontaneously reborn beings; there are no contemplatives or brahmans who, faring rightly & practicing rightly, proclaim this world & the next after having directly known & realized it for themselves.’ …
dn33:3.2.67: “Or he is born in the Middle Country but he is undiscerning, dull, drooling, incapable of knowing the meaning of what is well-said or ill-said.
dn33:3.2.68: This is the eighth inopportune, untimely situation for leading the holy life.
dn33:3.2.69: “And further, a Tathāgata does not appear in the world, worthy & rightly self-awakened. The Dhamma is not taught that leads to stilling, to total unbinding, to self-awakening as declared by One Well-gone.
dn33:3.2.70: This person is born in the Middle Country and is discerning, not dull, not drooling, capable of knowing the meaning of what is well-said or ill-said.
dn33:3.2.71: This is the ninth inopportune, untimely situation for leading the holy life.
dn33:3.2.73: There is the case, friends, where a monk, quite secluded from sensuality, secluded from unskillful qualities, enters & remains in the first jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of seclusion, accompanied by directed thought & evaluation.
dn33:3.2.74: “With the stilling of directed thoughts & evaluations, he enters & remains in the second jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of concentration, unification of awareness free from directed thought & evaluation—internal assurance.
dn33:3.2.75: “With the fading of rapture, he remains equanimous, mindful, & alert, and senses pleasure with the body. He enters & remains in the third jhāna, of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous & mindful, he has a pleasant abiding.’
dn33:3.2.76: “With the abandoning of pleasure & pain—as with the earlier disappearance of joy & distress—he enters & remains in the fourth jhāna: purity of equanimity & mindfulness, neither pleasure nor pain.
dn33:3.2.77: “With the complete transcending of perceptions of (physical) form, with the disappearance of perceptions of resistance, and not attending to perceptions of multiplicity, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite space,’ he enters & remains in the dimension of the infinitude of space.
dn33:3.2.78: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of space, (perceiving,) ‘Infinite consciousness,’ he enters & remains in the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness.
dn33:3.2.79: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness, (perceiving,) ‘There is nothing,’ he enters & remains in the dimension of nothingness.
dn33:3.2.80: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of nothingness, he enters & remains in the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.
dn33:3.2.81: “With the complete transcending of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, he enters & remains in the cessation of perception & feeling.
dn33:3.2.83: When one has attained the first jhāna, the perception of sensuality has ceased.
dn33:3.2.84: When one has attained the second jhāna, directed thoughts & evaluations [verbal fabrications] have ceased.
dn33:3.2.85: When one has attained the third jhāna, rapture has ceased.
dn33:3.2.86: When one has attained the fourth jhāna, in-&-out breaths [bodily fabrications] have ceased.
dn33:3.2.87: When one has attained the dimension of the infinitude of space, the perception of forms has ceased.
dn33:3.2.88: When one has attained the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness, the perception of the dimension of the infinitude of space has ceased.
dn33:3.2.89: When one has attained the dimension of nothingness, the perception of the dimension of the infinitude of consciousness has ceased.
dn33:3.2.90: When one has attained the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception, the perception of the dimension of nothingness has ceased.
dn33:3.2.91: When one has attained the cessation of perception & feeling, perceptions & feelings [mental fabrications] have ceased.
dn33:3.2.92: “These, friends, are the nine dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:3.2.93: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:3.3.1: “There are, friends, ten dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:3.3.2: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.
dn33:3.3.5: There is the case, friends, where a monk is virtuous. He dwells restrained in accordance with the Pāṭimokkha, consummate in his behavior & sphere of activity. He trains himself, having undertaken the training rules, seeing danger in the slightest faults.
dn33:3.3.6: And the fact that he is virtuous… seeing danger in the slightest faults
dn33:3.3.7: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.8: “And further, the monk has heard much, has retained what he has heard, has stored what he has heard. Whatever teachings are admirable in the beginning, admirable in the middle, admirable in the end, that—in their meaning and expression—proclaim the holy life that is entirely perfect, surpassingly pure: Those he has listened to often, retained, discussed, accumulated, examined with his mind, & well-penetrated in terms of his views.
dn33:3.3.9: And the fact that he has heard much… well-penetrated in terms of his views
dn33:3.3.10: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.11: “And further, the monk has admirable friends, admirable comrades, admirable companions.
dn33:3.3.12: And the fact that he has admirable friends, admirable comrades, admirable companions
dn33:3.3.13: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.14: “And further, the monk is easy to speak to, endowed with qualities that make him easy to speak to, patient, respectful to instruction.
dn33:3.3.15: And the fact that he is easy to speak to… respectful to instruction
dn33:3.3.16: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.17: “And further, the monk is adept at the various affairs involving his companions in the holy life, is vigorous, quick-witted in the techniques involved in them, is up to doing them or arranging to get them done.
dn33:3.3.18: And the fact that he is adept at… doing them or arranging to get them done
dn33:3.3.19: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.20: “And further, the monk is one who desires the Dhamma, endearing in his conversation, greatly rejoicing in the higher Dhamma & higher Vinaya.
dn33:3.3.21: And the fact that he is one who desires the Dhamma, endearing in his conversation, greatly rejoicing in the higher Dhamma & higher Vinaya
dn33:3.3.22: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.23: “And further, the monk is content with any old robe cloth at all, any old alms food, any old lodging, any old medicinal requisites for curing sickness at all.
dn33:3.3.24: And the fact that he is content with any old robe cloth at all, any old alms food, any old lodging, any old medicinal requisites for curing sickness at all
dn33:3.3.25: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.26: “And further, the monk keeps his persistence aroused for abandoning unskillful qualities and for taking on skillful qualities. He is steadfast, solid in his effort, not shirking his duties with regard to skillful qualities.
dn33:3.3.27: And the fact that he keeps his persistence aroused… not shirking his duties with regard to skillful qualities
dn33:3.3.28: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.29: “And further, the monk is mindful, endowed with excellent proficiency in mindfulness, remembering & recollecting what was done & said a long time ago.
dn33:3.3.30: And the fact that he is mindful, endowed with excellent proficiency in mindfulness, remembering & recollecting what was done & said a long time ago
dn33:3.3.31: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.32: “And further, the monk is discerning, endowed with discernment of arising & passing away—noble, penetrating, leading to the right ending of stress.
dn33:3.3.33: And the fact that the monk is discerning, endowed with discernment of arising & passing away—noble, penetrating, leading to the right ending of stress—
dn33:3.3.34: is a quality creating a protector.
dn33:3.3.36: One perceives the earth-totality above, below, all-around: non-dual [advayaṁ], immeasurable.
dn33:3.3.45: the consciousness-totality above, below, all-around: non-dual, immeasurable.
dn33:3.3.47: taking life, taking what is not given, sexual misconduct, telling lies, divisive speech, harsh speech, idle chatter, greed, ill will, wrong view.
dn33:3.3.49: refraining from taking life, refraining from taking what is not given, refraining from sexual misconduct, refraining from telling lies, refraining from divisive speech, refraining from harsh speech, refraining from idle chatter, non-greed, non-ill will, right view.
dn33:3.3.51: There is the case, friends, where a monk has abandoned five factors, is endowed with six, guards one, is supported in four, has shaken off idiosyncratic truths, has thoroughly given up searching, is undisturbed in his resolves, is calmed in his bodily-fabrication, is well-released in mind, is well-released in discernment.
dn33:3.3.52: “And how has a monk abandoned five factors?
dn33:3.3.53: There is the case where a monk’s sensual desire is abandoned. His ill will… His sloth & torpor… His restlessness & anxiety… His uncertainty is abandoned.
dn33:3.3.54: This is how a monk has abandoned five factors.
dn33:3.3.55: “And how is a monk endowed with six (factors)?
dn33:3.3.56: There is the case where a monk, on seeing a form via the eye, is neither gladdened nor saddened, but remains equanimous, mindful, & alert.
dn33:3.3.57: On hearing a sound via the ear… On smelling an aroma via the nose… On tasting a flavor via the tongue… On touching a tactile sensation via the body…
dn33:3.3.58: On cognizing an idea via the intellect, he is neither gladdened nor saddened, but remains equanimous, mindful, & alert.
dn33:3.3.59: This is how a monk is endowed with six (factors).
dn33:3.3.60: “And how does a monk guard one (factor)?
dn33:3.3.61: There is the case where a monk is endowed with an awareness guarded by mindfulness.
dn33:3.3.62: This is how a monk guards one (factor).
dn33:3.3.63: “And how is a monk supported in four (ways)?
dn33:3.3.64: There is the case where a monk, carefully reflecting, associates with one thing, acquiesces to another, avoids another, and subdues another.
dn33:3.3.65: This is how a monk is supported in four (ways).
dn33:3.3.66: “And how has a monk shaken off idiosyncratic truths [pacceka-sacca]?
dn33:3.3.67: There is the case where a monk has shaken off the run-of-the-mill idiosyncratic truths of run-of-the-mill contemplatives & brahmans—in other words, ‘The cosmos is eternal,’ ‘The cosmos is not eternal,’ ‘The cosmos is finite,’ ‘The cosmos is infinite,’ ‘The soul & the body are the same,’ ‘The soul is one thing and the body another,’ ‘After death a Tathāgata exists,’ ‘After death a Tathāgata does not exist,’ ‘After death a Tathāgata both does & does not exist,’ ‘After death a Tathāgata neither does nor does not exist.’ All of these he has thrown off, shaken off, renounced, vomited up, let go, abandoned, relinquished.
dn33:3.3.68: This is how a monk has shaken off idiosyncratic truths.
dn33:3.3.69: “And how has a monk thoroughly given up searching?
dn33:3.3.70: There is the case where a monk has abandoned his search for sensuality, has abandoned his search for becoming, and has allayed his search for a holy life.
dn33:3.3.71: This is how a monk has thoroughly given up searching.
dn33:3.3.72: “And how is a monk undisturbed in his resolves?
dn33:3.3.73: There is the case where a monk has abandoned his resolve for sensuality… his resolve for ill-will… his resolve for harmfulness.
dn33:3.3.74: This is how a monk is undisturbed in his resolves.
dn33:3.3.75: “And how is a monk calmed in his bodily fabrication?
dn33:3.3.76: There is the case where a monk, with the abandoning of pleasure & pain—as with the earlier disappearance of elation & distress—enters & remains in the fourth jhāna: purity of equanimity & mindfulness, neither pleasure nor pain.
dn33:3.3.77: This is how a monk is calmed in his bodily fabrication.
dn33:3.3.78: “And how is a monk well-released in mind?
dn33:3.3.79: There is the case where a monk’s mind is released from passion, released from aversion, released from delusion.
dn33:3.3.80: This is how a monk is well-released in mind.
dn33:3.3.81: “And how is a monk well-released in discernment?
dn33:3.3.82: There is the case where a monk discerns, ‘Passion is abandoned in me, its root destroyed, made like a palmyra stump, deprived of the conditions of development, not destined for future arising.’
dn33:3.3.83: He discerns, ‘Aversion is abandoned in me, its root destroyed, made like a palmyra stump, deprived of the conditions of development, not destined for future arising.’
dn33:3.3.84: He discerns, ‘Delusion is abandoned in me, its root destroyed, made like a palmyra stump, deprived of the conditions of development, not destined for future arising.’
dn33:3.3.85: This is how a monk is well-released in discernment.
dn33:3.3.87: the right view of one beyond training, the right resolve of one beyond training, the right speech of one beyond training, the right action of one beyond training, the right livelihood of one beyond training, the right effort of one beyond training, the right mindfulness of one beyond training, the right concentration of one beyond training, the right knowledge of one beyond training, the right release of one beyond training.
dn33:3.3.88: “These, friends, are the ten dhammas that are rightly expounded by the Blessed One—the one who knows, the one who sees, the Worthy One, rightly self-awakened.
dn33:3.3.89: Those should all be recited together, without dispute, so that this holy life will endure & last a long time, and that will be for the benefit & happiness of many, out of sympathy for the world, for the welfare, benefit, & happiness of devas & human beings.”
dn33:3.4.1: Then the Blessed One, having gotten up, said to Ven. Sāriputta,
dn33:3.4.3: It’s excellent how you have spoken to the monks of the sequence for reciting together.”
dn33:3.4.4: That is what Ven. Sāriputta said, the Teacher approved,
dn33:3.4.5: and gratified, the monks delighted in Ven.  Sāriputta’s words.
